/home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-xj80qu1k/PKGBUILD: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD

data.reasoning: Both models skipped the PKGBUILD entirely, misclassifying it as a 'binary file'. No actual content was reviewed by either model. The verdict cannot be determined as no analysis took place. This is a tooling defect, not a security finding — the PKGBUILD is a text file that was incorrectly flagged as binary, likely due to encoding detection issues (e.g., non-UTF-8 characters, unusual line endings, or a heuristic false trigger).
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no genuine disagreement — both models produced identical 'skipped' results due to the same tooling-level binary-file misdetection. Neither model exercised any judgment; both halted at the initial PKGBUILD safety check.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed by any model — the single most important file for an AUR package audit was skipped.
No .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts were enumerated or reviewed (cannot confirm whether others exist beyond PKGBUILD).
Zero files reviewed total across both reports.
false_positives: Both models flagged PKGBUILD as a 'binary file' — almost certainly a false detection. PKGBUILDs are shell scripts (plain text); the binary classifier appears to have a false-trigger condition.
false_negatives: Unknown — since the PKGBUILD content was never examined, any genuine risks (e.g., untrusted source URLs, curl|bash patterns in build/package, malicious .install hooks) would have been entirely missed.
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — must be forced through text analysis; investigate why it was classified as binary (encoding, BOM, control chars).
Any .install scripts and the source array / download URLs for the CLion EAP GDB binary distribution.
Verify integrity/checksums and source domains, since this package likely pulls a prebuilt GDB/CLion binary.
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic is producing false positives on plain-text PKGBUILD files. Add a hard override: files named 'PKGBUILD' and '.install' should always be treated as text and never skipped as binary.
When the initial PKGBUILD safety check fails due to a skip (not a content finding), the tool should fall back to a forced text read rather than aborting the whole audit.
A 'skipped' result with 0 files reviewed should never be aggregated as a usable verdict — surface it distinctly as a tooling failure requiring re-run.
Consider logging the byte/encoding reason a file was deemed binary so the root cause (BOM, NUL bytes, non-UTF8) is diagnosable.
Triggering re-audit on 'shallow (0 files reviewed)' was correct; this gate should remain and ideally auto-retry with the text override before escalating to a stronger model.

data.reasoning: All audit models incorrectly skipped the PKGBUILD file by misclassifying it as binary, preventing any security analysis. Since PKGBUILD is the only critical file defining build/install behavior, no meaningful assessment could be performed.
data.disagreement_analysis: No substantive disagreement - all models failed identically by skipping PKGBUILD due to faulty binary detection.
coverage_issues: Critical file PKGBUILD was skipped by all models due to erroneous binary file classification
No other files exist in this package, resulting in 0% coverage of executable logic
false_negatives: Entire package risk profile remains unknown since PKGBUILD wasn't analyzed
Potential risks in build/install scripts, network access, or filesystem operations were completely missed
learnings: Fix binary detection heuristics to never skip PKGBUILD files
Implement fallback mechanism: if PKGBUILD is skipped, treat as critical error
Prioritize PKGBUILD analysis above all other files
Add validation step to confirm at least PKGBUILD was successfully read
Improve error handling for file reading failures during initial audit
